item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! The entry was also inspired by Martin's negative opinion of fanfiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Makes me wonder what would happen if I took one; rights management is part of my job. At the same time, a larger public-domain would make my job SO much easier!

+1

P.S. Really enjoyed the parallel drawn between Lear and GoT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this quite a bit. At first I worried it was too similar to 1230, but this goes in a different direction and that last entry was very interesting. Good skip. I want a bottle of these pills in my medicine cabinet next to a bottle of 2022

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest I don't have any ideas for them at the moment after this. :P I'm sure I'll think of something, but the nature of the group means the topics I can choose for SCP material are inherently limited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm all for making a little bit more out of the PDPS. I really enjoyed 3185 and its related tale, and I really enjoy this as well. Not only does it have one of my favorite SCP tropes, a mostly benign but interesting test log with a whammy at the end of it, but the concept of the PDPS in general just tickles me.

It did take that last test log to tip me over into upvote territory, but that's fine. A really nice anomaly all the same. I am left wondering just how creative PDPS-releated objects could be, though.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was iffy about this at first then I got to the addendum and i realied how good this was

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It might work better if you printed the picture of a pill bottle out, put it on an actual pill bottle, then put up a picture of that.

But hey, +1 regardless! I wasn't sure I was gonna really get into this at first, but the test log definitely put it over the top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think taking out the bit about the pain — and refocusing on the loss of memories (both relevant to their copyright work, and to the consumption of public domain works) — makes it feel much less fetishistic, which is the main thing that bugged me about it when I read it.

The ending still feels murky to me, but it's hard for me to tell how much of that is actual murkiness and how much is just my initial impression still lingering on. I'll try rereading it later, after that impression has faded — so I can give it the thought it deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's intentionally redundant and bland-looking.

EDIT: I imagine the PDPS as having a very tone deaf and plain-looking aesthetic, so a pill bottle with a picture of a pill bottle seemed fitting (also works as an Inception joke)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pill bottles have pictures of pill bottles on them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How does this work as an alternate ending?

+ Show
- Hide

As the papers burn, the courtroom audience repeatedly chants the word "SUPPRESS", and the subject experiences discomfort in his perceived skin. As each document burns, the subject loses knowledge of the contents contained therein.

EDIT: Went ahead and changed the ending up, since I was not satisfied with it to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are certain elements of this that work for me, and other elements that don't.

The idea of dream pills that allow you to experience fiction with near-eidetic clarity is fun; the idea that it only shares 'public' fiction is also fun — and the idea that it somehow punishes those who suppress or restrict access to fiction is, while a bit predictable, also potentially interesting.

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-3285 will anomalously refill itself when the amount of pills in the bottle has fallen below five.

This seems unnecessary; why add another anomalous effect to what's already an anomaly? This might be an aesthetic difference, though — my preference is for anomalies with as few anomalous traits as possible.

Subjects affected by SCP-3285-A do not require sustenance to survive, and will awaken at the conclusion of an SCP-3285-A episode without displaying any expected negative physical side effects such as dehydration, malnourishment, or muscle atrophy.

Again, this seemed like an extra part of the anomaly that didn't contribute to the core story, and served to make the anomaly itself feel less 'credible' to me — why not just have them make you sleep for 7-8 hours? Particularly given the length of the dreams themselves aren't much longer than that. But, again, this might be chalked up to an aesthetic difference.

I think my bigger issue was with the end. For one thing, it just boils down to them being put on trial and tortured — I was expecting something along the lines of 'poetic justice' (like them being incapable of perceiving or otherwise consuming any text that's public domain; this includes legal documents — or them having to experience 8 hours of silence and a 'CENSORED BECAUSE OF COPYRIGHT' screen), but ultimately all the pill does is torture you.

On a broader scale, there's also the fact that I don't know how to feel about any of this: Someone's made a pill that gives us access to fiction in the public domain, but ultimately tortures you for six hours if you've contributed to restricting access to fiction. If the torture had a certain poetry to it, I might feel impressed at their cleverness — but as it boils down to just a thing that makes you experience pain while putting you through a kangaroo court that tells you how awful you are, all I'm left with is a mild revulsion to whoever created this thing.

TL;DR — this reads like 'AWCY gone petty and focused exclusively on the absurdity of copyright law'. Pettiness can work, but it's got to be clever — there has to be an elegance and poetry to the cruelty; otherwise, it starts to feel fetishistic.

Regardless, the polished technical components of the writing — combined with the creativity of the idea behind 'fiction in pill form' — is enough to prevent me from downvoting. Good luck with the article!

item.title: Public Domain Protection Service II: Copyright Boogaloo
item.content:encoded:
Testing the waters to see if I can get the PDPS to stick as more than just a one-off. This was originally going to be Keter, but it ended up feeling contrived so I stuck with my comfort zone. SCP-3185 is useful but not required reading.

Credit to SCP-3922 for style inspiration.

Image Source
